bio.education: Morningside High School University of Southern California
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Christine Leslie-Espinoza
  • Has two sisters and four half brothers
  • Married Michael Lockwood in 2005, and has two children, Lauren Jolie and Michael Joseph
  • Graduate of Morningside High School in California, where in 1998, she dedicated the Lisa Leslie Sports Complex, which includes a 42,000 square foot court with 12 hoops
  • Established the Lisa Leslie Basketball & Leadership Academy in 2012
  • Is a three-time ESPY Award Winner for Best WNBA Player and two-time Women’s Sports Foundation Sportswoman of the Year
  • After retiring from basketball in 2009, became the first WNBA player turned owner as co-owner of the Los Angeles Sparks
  • Has worked with foster children in Los Angeles and was presented with the 1999 Young Heroes Award from the Big Sisters Guild of Los Angeles
  • Served as a color commentator for USC basketball and has also been a guest correspondent on national networks, including ABC, Turner, Fox Sports and NBC, including supporting NBC’s coverage of the Olympic Games in 2012.
Olympic Experience
  • 4-time Olympian; 4-time Olympic medalist (4 gold)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2008, gold (Team - Women)
    • Olympic Games Athens 2004, gold (Team - Women)
    • Olympic Games Sydney 2000, gold (Team - Women)
    • Olympic Games Atlanta 1996, gold (Team - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2002 – gold (Team - Women)
  • Years of participation: Team - Women 1994, 1998, 2002
  • Medals: 3 (2 gold, 1 bronze)
    • Gold – 2002 (Team - Women); 1998 (Team - Women)
    • Bronze – 1994 (Team - Women)

Quick Facts
  • Started skating at age 2
  •  Calls short track speedskating a combination of two sports:  “demolition derby and NASCAR.”
Biographical Information 

Julie Letai started skating around two years old, following her two older siblings who were already ice skating at her local rink. She started in figure skating lessons at first before switching to speedskating around age 7.

She made her first U.S. national team in 2019 and has competed at four world championships for Team USA, including winning a silver medal on the women’s relay team at the 2024 world championships. Letai made her Olympic debut at the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, competing in the women’s 1500m and the women’s relay.

Off the ice, she hopes to pursue a career in healthcare after her speedskating career ends.

Olympic Experience
  • 2-time Olympian
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, 8th (2000m Relay - Mixed, 3000m Relay - Women), 16th (500m - Women), 28th (1000m - Women)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, 8th (3000m Relay - Women), 21th (1500m - Women)
World Championships Experience

  • Years of participation: 1000m - Women 2024, 2025; 2000m Relay - Mixed 2023, 2025; 500m - Women 2024, 2025; 1500m - Women 2024; 3000m Relay - Women 2021, 2023, 2024
  • Medals: 1 (1 silver)
    • Silver – 2024 (3000m Relay - Women)

bio.fun_fact: During the off-season is an assistant golf coach at her alma mater, Middlebury College, where she was a two-time conference champion in women's golf
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of James and Mary Anne Levins
  • Has three siblings, Augie, Jimmy and Keely
  • Won two state championships in golf in high school and was the Vermont State Amateur Champion in 2019
  • Assistant golf coach at Middlebury College, her alma mater, where she was a two-time conference champion and conference player of the year
  • Hobbies: golf, cycling and reading
Biographical Information

Chloe Levins grew up in Vermont hiking, biking and skiing around the Green Mountains. Levins excelled at golf and was a two-time high school state champion. She attended Middlebury College, where she continued to play golf as a two-time captain, two-time conference champion, and conference player of the year, all while pursuing her Olympic dreams in biathlon.

At age 17, Levins competed at the Youth Winter Olympic Games Lillehammer 2016, finishing just off the podium in fourth place in the 7.5-kilometer pursuit. The fourth-place finish marked the best for an American at the Youth Winter Olympics or Winter Olympics in the sport. 

Levins has represented the United States at four world championships in biathlon, including the mixed relay and team relay, as well as multiple individual events. 

During her off-season, Levins is an assistant coach for the Middlebury College golf team and enjoys exploring gravel and mountain biking trails around Vermont. Levins graduated from Middlebury with a degree in Neuroscience and Buddhism.

World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – 19th (4 x 6km Relay - Mixed, 4 x 6km Relay - Women), 41th (15km Individual - Women), 48th (10km Pursuit - Women), 52th (7.5km Sprint - Women)
  • Years of participation: 10km Pursuit - Women 2025; 15km Individual - Women 2021, 2023, 2024, 2025; 4 x 6km Relay - Mixed 2024, 2025; 4 x 6km Relay - Women 2023, 2024, 2025; 7.5km Sprint - Women 2023, 2024, 2025
  • Top finish: 11th – 2024 (4 x 6km Relay - Mixed)
Career Highlights
  • World Youth/Junior Championships, Lenzerheide, 2020 (4 x 6km Relay - Women, 10km Pursuit - Women, 12.5km Individual - Women, 7.5km Sprint - Women)
  • World Youth/Junior Championships, Brezno, 2019 (10km Pursuit - Women)

bio.fun_fact: Named after Michelle Pfeiffer’s character in the 1985 movie, Ladyhawke
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Specialty: Women's Singles 
  • Daughter of Chiara Garberi, who moved to the U.S. from Italy in 1997
  • Named after Michelle Pfeiffer’s character in the 1985 movie, Ladyhawke
  • Enjoys reading, brings up to 10 books on trips
  • Her favorite food is pasta
Biographical Information

Isabeau Levito became interested in figure skating at three after watching the Olympic Winter Games Vancouver 2010. Her mother, Chiara Garberi, put Levito into ice skating at first as a reward for finishing her meals and also to strengthen her balance. Levito immediately took to the sport, and less than eight years later became the 2018 U.S. juvenile champion. 

At age 14, Levito finished third at the U.S. Championships and, three months later, became world junior champion. She was the first U.S. female figure skater to win that title in 14 years. Her early success on the national and international stages established her as a promising talent in the sport.

In subsequent years, Levito won the 2023 U.S. Championships and a silver medal at the 2024 ISU World Figure Skating Championships. 

Outside of skating, she enjoys watching Gilmore Girls, listening to music from artists like Nicki Minaj and Lana Del Rey, and indulging in Italian cuisine.

World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – 4th (Singles - Women)
  • Years of participation: Singles - Women 2023, 2024, 2025
  • Medals: 1 (1 silver)
    • Silver – 2024 (Singles - Women)

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daugher of Dale and Carol Lewis
  • Married Gerrod Chadwell on August 6, 2016, with the couple honeymooning in Rio during the 2016 Olympic Games
  • From the age of 11, wore a back brace 18 hours per day for 7-1/2 years to correct curvature in her spine from scoliosis, removing it only to play golf. Had back surgery during her senior year of high school and redshirted for one season before joining the University of Arkansas women’s golf team
  • Hobbies include working out, watching any sports especially SEC football, traveling and having spare time to herself.

Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Warren Li and Amy Zeng
  • Has one brother, Luke, who works for a financial firm on Wall Street
  • Li started playing golf at age 7 while her brother was hitting balls on the range
  • Prior to golf, Li took diving lessons at Stanford University and was even diving from the 10-meter platform at age 4
  • She also participated in gymnastics and music
  • Li calls herself a “history buff and bookworm and huge NBA fan” on her Twitter profile.
Career Highlights
  • 2018 U.S. Women’s Open, youngest player in the field and one of seven amateurs to make the cut (finished T55)
  • 2017 ANA Inspiration, youngest player in the field and only amateur to make the cut (finished T70)
  • 2017 First Team AJGA (American Junior Golf Association) Rolex Junior All-American
  • 2016 Junior PGA Championship (won)
  • 2014 U.S. Women’s Open, youngest qualifier in the event’s history at 11 years old, surpassing Lexi Thompson’s record of 12 years, 4 months, 8 days

bio.education: The Winter Sports School (Park City, Utah) '02
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Son of Bill Ligety and Cydni Sharp
  • Has one brother, Charly
  • Has one son, Jax, with his wife Mia
  • Began skiing at 2 and racing at 11
  • Founded Shred Optics, which produces helmets, goggles, sunglasses, protection and gloves for skiers and cyclists
  • Hobbies include playing tennis and badminton, water skiing and watching formula one racing.
Career Highlights
  • 2015 FIS World Cup Standings, 3rd (giant slalom)
  • 2014 FIS World Cup Standings, 1st (giant slalom, combined)
  • 2013 FIS World Cup Standings, 1st (giant slalom), 3rd (overall)
  • 2012 FIS World Cup Standings, 2nd (giant slalom)
  • 2011 FIS World Cup Standings, 1st (giant slalom)
  • 2010 FIS World Cup Standings, 1st (giant slalom)
  • 2009 FIS World Cup Standings, 1st (giant slalom), 3rd (giant slalom)
  • In 2013, became the first skier to earn three gold medals at a single world championship since Jean Claude Killy in 1968
Olympic Experience
  • 4-time Olympian; 2-time Olympic medalist (2 gold)
    • Olympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, (Super G - Men), 5th (Super Combined - Men), 15th (Giant Slalom - Men)
    • Olympic Games Sochi 2014, (Slalom - Men), gold (Giant Slalom - Men), 12th (Super Combined - Men), 14th (Super G - Men)
    • Olympic Games Vancouver 2010, (Slalom - Men), 5th (Super Combined - Men), 9th (Giant Slalom - Men), 19th (Super G - Men)
    • Olympic Games Turin 2006, (Giant Slalom - Men, Slalom - Men), gold (Combination - Men)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2019 – (Super Combined - Men), 11th (Giant Slalom - Men)
  • Years of participation: Giant Slalom - Men 2007, 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015, 2019; Super Combined - Men 2007, 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015, 2019; Slalom - Men 2005, 2007, 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015; Super G - Men 2007, 2009, 2011, 2013, 2015; Team - Mixed 2005, 2007, 2011, 2015; Combination - Men 2005
  • Medals: 7 (5 gold, 2 bronze)
    • Gold – 2015 (Giant Slalom - Men); 2013 (Giant Slalom - Men, Super Combined - Men, Super G - Men); 2011 (Giant Slalom - Men)
    • Bronze – 2015 (Super Combined - Men); 2009 (Giant Slalom - Men)
